title: The NULL Trap
short_description: A NOT IN filter returns no rows because the subquery contains a NULL customer key.
business_context: The CRM team wants a list of customers who have never placed an order so they can launch a reactivation campaign. An analyst wrote a simple anti-join query, but the result set is empty even though the business knows there are inactive customers.
problem_statement: The anti-join logic uses `NOT IN` against the orders table. Unfortunately, one bad order row has a NULL `customer_id`, and the filter now behaves unexpectedly. Use the sample tables to return the customers who truly never placed an order.
student_task: Write a single read-only SQL query that returns the customers who never placed an order. Match the expected output columns exactly.
learning_goals: Understand why NULL values break naive `NOT IN` anti-joins.
Use `NOT EXISTS` for safer anti-join logic.
Recognize production data quality issues hiding in reference keys.
production_logs: 2026-05-15 08:02:41 WARN orders.customer_id contains 1 NULL value from a malformed order event
solution_query:
SELECT c.customer_id, c.customer_name
FROM customers AS c
WHERE NOT EXISTS (
  SELECT 1
  FROM orders AS o
  WHERE o.customer_id = c.customer_id
)
explanation: The NULL in `orders.customer_id` poisons the `NOT IN` comparison, so the predicate never evaluates to true for any customer. `NOT EXISTS` is safer because it checks row-by-row correlation instead of comparing against a set that includes NULL.
common_mistakes: Using `NOT IN` without first filtering NULLs out of the subquery.
Converting the query to an inner join and losing the anti-join behavior entirely.
Returning only customer IDs and forgetting the campaign needs customer names too.
hints: Look for suspicious values in the orders table before trusting the anti-join.
Ask what `NOT IN` does when one of the compared values is NULL.
A correlated anti-join is often the safest pattern here.
